summary.intent: Fix IME (Input Method Editor) composition handling bugs in the Editable component of the ars-ui project, addressing three code review threads from Codex on PR #683.
summary.outcome: Successfully fixed IME composition handling across two review rounds (commits including 43498ac2 and a follow-up), resolving all six Codex review threads, passing full CI including proptest with 256 cases, fmt, clippy, spec validation, and coverage.

repo: The Editable component lives in crates/ars-components/src/input/editable/mod.rs and has an IME state machine with composing guards that must be applied consistently across both the runtime code and the proptest model.
Proptest state machine tests for editable event sequences live in crates/ars-components/tests/proptest_state_machines/input.rs and must mirror the same composition guards as the production machine.
The project uses a spec validation step (cargo xtask spec validate) that must pass alongside fmt, clippy, and coverage checks.
Codex review is triggered by posting '@codex review' as a PR comment and its status is tracked via GitHub reactions (eyes = active review) and review threads.
CI matrix includes: formatting, snapshots, workspace check, clippy, mutual-exclusion, unit tests, adapter tests, DOM browser tests, coverage, Dioxus feature-flag groups, and release verification.

code.finding: CompositionEnd must suppress the next Enter keypress as a possible IME confirmation key before allowing a subsequent Enter to submit. Submit and blur events must be ignored while IME composition is active. The retry path in the adapter is the only place that should suppress Enter post-composition.

The proptest model's EditableAction handling around lines 228-292 needed the same composing guards as the production state machine; without them the model and machine diverge and the property test can find spurious counterexamples.
clamp_editable_text and related helpers around lines 470-545 are part of the proptest infrastructure for the editable state machine.

workflow: Codex review threads are fetched via GitHub GraphQL API filtering on isResolved==false and author login matching 'codex'; replies are posted with gh api and threads resolved via resolveReviewThread mutation.
The polling loop (sleep 120 + check reactions + unresolved thread count + gh pr checks) is an effective pattern for monitoring slow CI and async bot reviews.
Running PROPTEST_CASES=256 with the --ignored flag is necessary to exercise the heavier proptest suite that normally doesn't run in standard CI.
cargo llvm-cov with --lib and a path filter provides targeted coverage reporting for a single module without running the full suite.

friction: Multiple polling cycles (8+) of 2-minute sleeps were required to wait for CI to fully complete, making the overall session very long.
Codex posted a second wave of three review threads after the first fix was merged, requiring a second full fix-test-commit cycle.
The CI matrix has many sequential stages (coverage, feature-flag groups, release verification last) that extend total wall-clock time significantly.
open_items: Coverage, Dioxus feature-flag groups, and release verification checks were still pending at several points in the session — their final status after the last push is not confirmed in the transcript.
The proptest regression test for normal Enter after composition end was added; its long-term adequacy as a regression guard should be monitored as the IME state machine evolves.
Codex had not posted a final approval reaction at session end — a follow-up check for the +1 or approval state may be needed.
